original:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 36110/97) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by four Turkish nationals, Mr Galip Yalman, Mr Bahattin Sarısoy, Mr Osman Çağlayan and Mr Yusuf Çamca (“the applicants”), on 29 November 1996.

The applicants were represented by Mr S. Esmer, a lawyer practising in Ankara. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) did not designate an Agent for the purposes of the proceedings before the Convention institutions.

The applicants alleged that their case, which commenced in 1989 and terminated in 1996, was not heard within a reasonable time as required by the Convention.

The application was transmitted to the Court on 1 November 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The application was allocated to the Second Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On 1 November 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed First Section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision of 15 May 2003, the Court declared the application admissible.

The applicants and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

In September 1980 the applicants were taken into police custody and were subsequently placed in detention on remand on suspicion of membership of an illegal organisation. The Erzincan Military Public Prosecutor filed a bill of indictment with the Erzincan Martial Law Court (sıkıyönetim askeri mahkemesi) charging the applicants under Article 146 of the Criminal Code with membership of an illegal organisation whose object was to undermine the constitutional order.

On various dates between 1982 and 1984 the applicants were released pending trial.

On 13 September 1988 the Erzincan Martial Law Court acquitted the applicants of the charges against them.

On various dates in March, April and June 1989 the applicants brought individual actions before the Sinop Assize Court against the Treasury, pursuant to Law no. 466. They requested compensation for their unjustified detention on remand.

On 20 October 1989 the court decided to join the applicants’ cases.

On 15 December 1993 the Sinop Assize Court awarded non‑pecuniary compensation to the applicants. The applicants appealed, arguing that the amount of compensation awarded was not sufficient.

On 31 January 1995 the Court of Cassation quashed the judgment of the first instance court.

On 6 June 1995 the Sinop Assize Court increased the amount of non‑pecuniary compensation.

On 15 June 1995 the applicants again appealed.

On 30 May 1996 the Court of Cassation dismissed the applicants’ appeal and upheld the judgment of the assize court.

By a decision of 15 May 2003, the Court declared the application admissible.

THE FACTS

In September 1980 the applicants were taken into police custody and were subsequently placed in detention on remand on suspicion of membership of an illegal organisation. On various dates between 1982 and 1984 the applicants were released pending trial.

On various dates in March, April and June 1989 the applicants brought individual actions before the Sinop Assize Court against the Treasury, pursuant to Law no. 466. They requested compensation for their unjustified detention on remand.

original:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in two applications (nos. 43185/98 and 43186/98) against the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by two United Kingdom nationals, John Harding Price (introduced on 4 May 1998) and Mary Hazel Lowe (introduced on 12 August 1998).

The United Kingdom Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr C.A. Whomersley of the Foreign and Commonwealth Office.

The applicants alleged that the proceedings in which they were sued in respect of property in Florida were not dealt with within a reasonable time.

The applications were transmitted to the Court on 1 November 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The applications were allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On 1 November 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Second Section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision of 3 September 2002 the Court declared the applications partly admissible, having in the meantime joined them on 28 November 2000.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in 1929 and 1923 respectively. The applicant in application no. 43185/98 (“Dr Price”) is a consultant psychiatrist; the applicant in application no. 43186/98 (“Mrs Lowe”) is a medical secretary. They live in Lincoln.

The applicants, together with Dr Price’s wife, were sued by “K”, a former patient of Dr Price, in respect of property in Florida. The writ was issued on 12 February 1986. Before the action came to trial, the plaintiff was adjudicated bankrupt on 9 August 1989 and the trustee in bankruptcy continued the action.

The trial was set down to commence in the High Court in August 1994 but was postponed until the end of September 1994 because the judge was not available for the length of time needed for the hearing.

On the fourth day of trial, while K was being cross-examined, an adjournment was granted because K claimed to feel unwell. In the event, it emerged that no medical justification could be given for his continued non-attendance, but nonetheless he remained absent for the rest of the trial. Because of the delay caused by K’s non-attendance, the trial could not be concluded in September 1994 and had to be adjourned until January 1995. Judgment was given on 27 April 1995.

The judge accepted the argument of the applicants and Mrs Price that it was necessary to disregard K’s evidence because of his failure to remain for cross-examination. However, on the basis of the documentary evidence and that adduced by the applicants and Mrs Price, the judge found that the transfer of the Florida property should be set aside on the ground that the defendants had not been able to rebut the presumption of undue influence created by the discrepancy in the price they paid K for the property and its value at the time of the transfer, together with the doctor-patient relationship between Dr Price and K.

The formal record of the judge’s order was not prepared by the parties until 26 October 1995. The applicants and Mrs Price applied to the Court of Appeal for leave to appeal on 1 November 1995. The appeal bundles were lodged on 4 October 1996. On 15 January 1997 the appeal was listed for hearing, and it was heard on 16 and 17 April 1997, on which last date the Court of Appeal gave judgment dismissing the appeal.

In connection with the moneys paid by Dr Price for the property, the Court of Appeal confirmed the decision of the trial judge that the sum should be repaid, but that no interest should be paid on it. The trial judge had based his decision on the fact that Dr Price “had had possession of the properties and receipt of the rents since the completion of the sale, subject only to the agreement to allow [K] to use number 212 in the early months”. Lord Justice Nourse regarded this as a “rough and ready result”, but one which would “reduce the opportunities for further dispute between the parties”.

On 19 April 1997 the applicants and Mrs Price were refused leave by the Court of Appeal to appeal to the House of Lords. On 30 March 1998 the applicants and Mrs Price were refused leave to appeal by the House of Lords.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in two applications (referred to as Application A and Application B) against a Member State lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by two nationals of that Member State, Applicant A (introduced on an initial date) and Applicant B (introduced on a subsequent date).

The Government of the Member State (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, a representative of a governmental department.

The applicants alleged that the proceedings in which they were sued in respect of property in an overseas location were not dealt with within a reasonable time.

The applications were transmitted to the Court on a particular date, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The applications were allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

Some years later the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Second Section (Rule 52 § 1).

By a decision of a later date the Court declared the applications partly admissible, having in the meantime joined them on an earlier date.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in earlier years. The applicant in Application A (referred to as Applicant A) is a medical professional; the applicant in Application B (referred to as Applicant B) is an administrative professional. They live in a certain locality.

The applicants, together with Applicant A's spouse, were sued by a plaintiff, a former patient of Applicant A, in respect of property in the overseas location. The writ was issued on an initial date. Before the action came to trial, the plaintiff was adjudicated bankrupt on a subsequent date and the trustee in bankruptcy continued the action.

The trial was set down to commence in the High Court in a specific month but was postponed until the following month because the presiding judge was not available for the length of time needed for the hearing.

On the fourth day of trial, while the plaintiff was being cross-examined, an adjournment was granted because the plaintiff claimed to feel unwell. In the event, it emerged that no medical justification could be given for the plaintiff's continued non-attendance, but nonetheless the plaintiff remained absent for the rest of the trial. Because of the delay caused by the plaintiff's non-attendance, the trial could not be concluded in the initial month and had to be adjourned until some months later. Judgment was given on a date in the spring.

The presiding judge accepted the argument of the applicants and Applicant A's spouse that it was necessary to disregard the plaintiff's evidence because of the plaintiff's failure to remain for cross-examination. However, on the basis of the documentary evidence and that adduced by the applicants and Applicant A's spouse, the presiding judge found that the transfer of the overseas location property should be set aside on the ground that the defendants had not been able to rebut the presumption of undue influence created by the discrepancy in the price they paid the plaintiff for the property and its value at the time of the transfer, together with the doctor-patient relationship between Applicant A and the plaintiff.

The formal record of the presiding judge’s order was not prepared by the parties until a date later that year. The applicants and Applicant A's spouse applied to the Court of Appeal for leave to appeal on a date in November. The appeal bundles were lodged on a date the following year. On a date in January the appeal was listed for hearing, and it was heard in mid-April, on which last date the Court of Appeal gave judgment dismissing the appeal.

In connection with the moneys paid by Applicant A for the property, the Court of Appeal confirmed the decision of the initial judge that the sum should be repaid, but that no interest should be paid on it. The initial judge had based the decision on the fact that Applicant A “had had possession of the properties and receipt of the rents since the completion of the sale, subject only to the agreement to allow the plaintiff to use a particular unit in the early months”. A senior judge regarded this as a “rough and ready result”, but one which would “reduce the opportunities for further dispute between the parties”.

On a few days later the applicants and Applicant A's spouse were refused leave by the Court of Appeal to appeal to the House of Lords. On a date in March the applicants and Applicant A's spouse were refused leave to appeal by the House of Lords.

original:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 42552/98) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by Turkish nationals, Mr Mehmet Bülent Yılmaz and Mr Şahin Yılmaz (“the applicants”), on 20 May 1998.

The applicants, who had been granted legal aid, were represented by Mr H.Ç. Akbulut, a lawyer practising in Izmir. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) did not designate an Agent for the purpose of the proceedings before the Court.

On 21 March 2002 the Court decided to communicate the application to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in 1975 and 1970 respectively.

On 19 and 20 August 1995 respectively the applicants were taken into custody by police officers from the Anti-Terrorism Department of the Aydın Security Directorate on suspicion of their membership to an illegal organisation, namely the DHKP/C.

In their police statements the applicants accepted the accusations against them.

On 22 August 1995 the applicants were brought before the investigating judge at the Aydın Magistrate’s Court, where they repeated the statements they had made to the police. The investigating judge ordered that they be placed in detention on remand.

In an indictment dated 6 September 1995, the public prosecutor at the Izmir State Security Court initiated criminal proceedings against the applicants. He charged the first applicant with being member of an armed gang under Article 168 § 2 of the Criminal Code and the second applicant with aiding and abetting an armed gang under Article 169 of the Criminal Code.

During the hearings before the Izmir State Security Court, the applicants denied the statements they had made to the police and the investigating judge.

On 25 March 1997 the Izmir State Security Court, composed of two civilian judges and a military judge, convicted the applicants as charged and sentenced the first applicant to three years and nine months’ imprisonment and the second applicant to twelve years and six months’ imprisonment. Neither the applicants nor their lawyers were present in this last hearing.

The applicants appealed. On 12 November 1997 the Court of Cassation dismissed the applicants’ appeal, upholding the Izmir State Security Court’s reasoning and assessment of evidence. The decision, which was pronounced in the absence of the applicants and their lawyers, was deposited with the Registry of the Izmir State Security Court on 21 November 1997.

The case originated in an application (no. an application number) against the Republic of Turkey lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by Turkish nationals, Applicant A and Applicant B (“the applicants”), on a particular date.

The applicants, who had been granted legal aid, were represented by their legal representative, a legal professional practising in a particular city. The Turkish Government (“the Government”) did not designate an Agent for the purpose of the proceedings before the Tribunal.

On a later date the Tribunal decided to communicate the application to the Government. Under the provisions of Article 29 § 3 of the Convention, it decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicants were born in an earlier year and a slightly earlier year respectively.

On two consecutive days in mid-August of an earlier year respectively the applicants were taken into custody by police officers from a specialized anti-terrorism department of a regional security directorate on suspicion of their membership to an illegal organisation, namely an illegal organization.

In their police statements the applicants accepted the accusations against them.

On a few days later the applicants were brought before a judicial official overseeing investigations at the Aydın Magistrate’s Court, where they repeated the statements they had made to the police. The judicial official overseeing investigations ordered that they be placed in detention on remand.

In an indictment dated a subsequent date, a prosecuting attorney at the Izmir State Security Court initiated criminal proceedings against the applicants. He charged the first applicant with being member of an armed gang under Article 168 § 2 of the Criminal Code and the second applicant with aiding and abetting an armed gang under Article 169 of the Criminal Code.

During the hearings before the Izmir State Security Court, the applicants denied the statements they had made to the police and the judicial official overseeing investigations.

On a later date the Izmir State Security Court, composed of two non-military judicial officials and a military judicial official, convicted the applicants as charged and sentenced the first applicant to a specific prison term and the second applicant to a significantly longer prison term. Neither the applicants nor their legal professionals were present in this last hearing.

The applicants appealed. On a further date the Court of Cassation dismissed the applicants’ appeal, upholding the Izmir State Security Court’s reasoning and assessment of evidence. The decision, which was pronounced in the absence of the applicants and their legal professionals, was deposited with the Registry of the Izmir State Security Court on another specific date.

original:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 5136/11) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, Mr Kamil Prus (“the applicant”), on 6 December 2010.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by Mr S. Kotuła, a lawyer practising in Lublin. The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Ms J. Chrzanowska, of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant alleged, in particular, a breach of Article 3 of the Convention on account of the imposition on him of the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime.

On 7 July 2014 the application was communicated to the Government.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1987 and is detained in Lublin.

A. Criminal proceedings against the applicant

On 1 December 2005 the applicant was arrested and remanded in custody. In 2006 he was convicted of three counts of battery and robbery and received prison sentences ranging from two to four years. On 28 November 2006 the Lublin Regional Court imposed a cumulative sentence for four criminal convictions.

B. Imposition of the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime

On 5 November 2010 the Opole Lubelskie Prison Penitentiary Commission imposed on the applicant the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime. The commission held that the applicant had been the leader of or an active participant in a planned collective remonstrance in Opole Lubelskie Prison (Article 88a § 2 (2a) of the Code of Execution of Criminal Sentences), as the detainees refused to accept food provided by the prison authorities. The authorities learned that the detainees were planning another collective protest for 11 November 2010. The commission also considered that the applicant was particularly depraved.

On 18 January 2011 the Opole Lubelskie Prison Penitentiary Commission rejected an appeal lodged by the applicant and dismissed his request for leave to examine the appeal outside the statutory time-limit.

Subsequently, the applicant was transferred to the Lublin Remand Centre.

On 3 February 2011 the Lublin Remand Centre Penitentiary Commission reviewed and upheld the decision to apply the regime to the applicant, considering that he posed a serious threat to the security of the detention centre. The applicant appealed.

On 4 April 2011 the Lublin Regional Court dismissed the appeal. The court referred to the reasons given in the decision of 5 November 2010, holding that the decision had been lawful and justified.

On 3 March 2011 the applicant lodged a complaint with the Lublin Regional Inspectorate of Prisons. He complained that he was having difficulties in accessing educational activities in the Lublin Remand Centre. The authorities dismissed the complaint, holding that educational activities were not organised for dangerous prisoners.

On an unspecified date in 2011 the applicant notified the police of his allegedly unlawful classification as a “dangerous detainee” on 5 November 2010. He claimed that the Opole Lubelskie Prison authorities had exceeded their powers in imposing the regime on him. On 29 April 2011 the Opole Lubelskie District Prosecutor refused to open an investigation in the case, holding that no offence had been committed.

On 28 April 2011 the Lublin Remand Centre Penitentiary Commission revoked its decision to classify the applicant as a “dangerous detainee”. It considered that the applicant’s behaviour had improved and that he no longer posed a threat to the security of the remand centre.

The case originated in an application (a specific application number) against the Republic of Poland lodged with the international human rights court under Article 34 of the relevant human rights convention (“the Convention”) by a Polish national, the applicant, on a given date.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by a legal representative, a lawyer practising in Lublin. The Polish Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, a representative from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant alleged, in particular, a breach of Article 3 of the Convention on account of the imposition on him of the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime.

On a subsequent date the application was communicated to the Government.

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in a given year and is detained in Lublin.

A. Criminal proceedings against the applicant

On an initial date the applicant was arrested and remanded in custody. In a subsequent year he was convicted of three counts of battery and robbery and received prison sentences ranging from two to four years. On a further date the Lublin Regional Court imposed a cumulative sentence for four criminal convictions.

B. Imposition of the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime

On a particular date the Opole Lubelskie Prison Penitentiary Commission imposed on the applicant the so-called “dangerous detainee” regime. The commission held that the applicant had been the leader of or an active participant in a planned collective remonstrance in Opole Lubelskie Prison (Article 88a § 2 (2a) of the Code of Execution of Criminal Sentences), as the detainees refused to accept food provided by the prison authorities. The authorities learned that the detainees were planning another collective protest for a later date. The commission also considered that the applicant was particularly depraved.

On a subsequent date the Opole Lubelskie Prison Penitentiary Commission rejected an appeal lodged by the applicant and dismissed his request for leave to examine the appeal outside the statutory time-limit.

Subsequently, the applicant was transferred to the Lublin Remand Centre.

On a further date the Lublin Remand Centre Penitentiary Commission reviewed and upheld the decision to apply the regime to the applicant, considering that he posed a serious threat to the security of the detention centre. The applicant appealed.

On another date the Lublin Regional Court dismissed the appeal. The court referred to the reasons given in a decision from an earlier date, holding that the decision had been lawful and justified.

On yet another date the applicant lodged a complaint with the Lublin Regional Inspectorate of Prisons. He complained that he was having difficulties in accessing educational activities in the Lublin Remand Centre. The authorities dismissed the complaint, holding that educational activities were not organised for dangerous prisoners.

On an unspecified date in a given year the applicant notified the police of his allegedly unlawful classification as a “dangerous detainee” on a particular prior date. He claimed that the Opole Lubelskie Prison authorities had exceeded their powers in imposing the regime on him. On a subsequent date the Opole Lubelskie District Prosecutor refused to open an investigation in the case, holding that no offence had been committed.

On a final date the Lublin Remand Centre Penitentiary Commission revoked its decision to classify the applicant as a “dangerous detainee”. It considered that the applicant’s behaviour had improved and that he no longer posed a threat to the security of the remand centre.

original:
PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 57836/00) against the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a United Kingdom national, Mr Spencer James Mellors (“the applicant”), on 12 January 2000.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by Mr J. Carroll, a lawyer practising in Glasgow. The United Kingdom Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr C. Whomersley of the Foreign and Commonwealth Office, London.

The applicant alleged that his criminal proceedings exceeded a reasonable time contrary to Article 6 § 1 of the Convention.

The application was allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On 19 June 2001, the Court rejected part of the application as inadmissible.

On 1 November 2001, the Court changed the composition of its sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Third Section.

By a final decision of 30 January 2003, the Court declared the application partly admissible.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). The Chamber decided, after consulting the parties, that no hearing on the merits was required (Rule 59 § 3 in fine).

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in 1964 and is currently serving a sentence of imprisonment in HM Prison Craiginches, Aberdeen.

The applicant was arrested on a charge of attempted murder arising out of a high speed car chase which occurred in the early hours of 29 October 1995. On 30 October 1995, he was arrested again, interviewed and cautioned in respect of a separate incident of rape and assault which also occurred during the early morning of 29 October 1995.

On 31 October 1995, the applicant appeared in court in respect of the various road traffic offences and the attempted murder of a police officer. On 7 November 1995, he was fully committed and remanded in custody on those charges (of which he was later acquitted).

On 30 November 1995, the applicant appeared before the Sheriff and was committed in respect of the rape charge.

On 24 June 1996, the trial began in the High Court. On 2 July 1996, the applicant was convicted, by a majority verdict, of all charges and sentenced to 9 years 9 months' imprisonment.

On 8 July 1996, the applicant lodged an intention to appeal with the High Court sitting as an appeal court. On 20 August 1996, a Note of Appeal was lodged.

On 26 September 1996, a single judge refused the appeal. The applicant appealed.

On 31 October 1996, the Note of Appeal was allowed by three judges in restricted terms relating to the direction given by the trial judge on the special defence of alibi.

On 20 December 1996, the appeal was set down to be heard. According to the Government, it did not take place as the applicant refused to leave prison. The applicant stated that contrary to statutory authority the prison governor refused to allow him to leave the prison wearing civilian clothes instead of a prison uniform for the hearing. A further hearing was fixed for 22 May 1997. On that date, the Crown requested, and obtained, an adjournment as the Advocate Depute was engaged abroad.

On 19 November 1997, the appeal was set down to be heard. It was discontinued due to lack of court time and pressure of other business. It was noted that the appeal was complex and would last half a day.

On 13 and 14 January 1998, there was another appeal hearing. As the applicant's solicitor had become aware that the prosecution had never cited a witness, W. to appear at the trial, he had lodged and argued an additional ground of appeal on this point. Following argument, he sought to lodge further additional grounds of appeal. The appeal was continued to 13 February 1998 to enable him to do so.

On 13 February 1998, the High Court held that the failure to hear the evidence of W. resulted in unfairness and that further evidence should be heard. Lord Philip was designated as the judge to hear the evidence. On 28 May 1998, he fixed a date to take the evidence of W. On 20 August 1998, the hearing was adjourned as the applicant's counsel withdrew from acting on his behalf.

On 16 October 1998, W. gave evidence. In his note of 20 November 1998, Lord Philip gave his view of the credibility and reliability of W. The appeal was relisted for hearing on 8 January 1999 but the applicant's solicitors wrote to the court requesting that another date, possibly in the following week, be fixed due to the unavailability of counsel.

On 24 June 1999, the appeal was heard before the High Court.

On 22 July 1999, the judgment of the court was given by the Lord Justice General, Lord Rodger. It dismissed the applicant's appeal.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. [CASE_NUMBER]) against the United Kingdom lodged with the European Court of Human Rights under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by a UK national, an individual referred to as “the applicant”, on a date in early 2000.

The applicant, who had been granted legal aid, was represented by a legal counsel practising in a city in Scotland. The UK Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, an official from the Foreign and Commonwealth Office, based in the capital city.

The applicant alleged that his criminal proceedings exceeded a reasonable time contrary to Article 6 § 1 of the Convention.

The application was allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

On a date in mid-2001, the Court rejected part of the application as inadmissible.

On a subsequent date in 2001, the Court changed the composition of its sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed Third Section.

By a final decision of 30 January 2003, the Court declared the application partly admissible.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1). The Chamber decided, after consulting the parties, that no hearing on the merits was required (Rule 59 § 3 in fine).

THE FACTS

The applicant was born in the mid-1960s and is currently serving a sentence of imprisonment in a correctional facility in a northern city.

The applicant was arrested on a charge of attempted murder arising out of a high speed car chase which occurred in the early hours of a late October day in 1995. On the following day, he was arrested again, interviewed and cautioned in respect of a separate incident of rape and assault which also occurred during the early morning of the same late October day in 1995.

On a further date in late October 1995, the applicant appeared in court in respect of the various road traffic offences and the attempted murder of a police officer. On a date in early November 1995, he was fully committed and remanded in custody on those charges (of which he was later acquitted).

On a late November date in 1995, the applicant appeared before a Sheriff and was committed in respect of the rape charge.

On a date in mid-1996, the trial began in the High Court. On an early July date in 1996, the applicant was convicted, by a majority verdict, of all charges and sentenced to 9 years 9 months' imprisonment.

On a mid-July date in 1996, the applicant lodged an intention to appeal with the High Court sitting as an appeal court. On a late August date in 1996, a Note of Appeal was lodged.

On a late September date in 1996, a single judge refused the appeal. The applicant appealed.

On a late October date in 1996, the Note of Appeal was allowed by three judges in restricted terms relating to the direction given by the trial judge on the special defence of alibi.

On a late December date in 1996, the appeal was set down to be heard. According to the Government, it did not take place as the applicant refused to leave prison. The applicant stated that contrary to statutory authority the prison governor refused to allow him to leave the prison wearing civilian clothes instead of a prison uniform for the hearing. A further hearing was fixed for a date in late May 1997. On that date, the Crown requested, and obtained, an adjournment as the Advocate Depute was engaged abroad.

On a late November date in 1997, the appeal was set down to be heard. It was discontinued due to lack of court time and pressure of other business. It was noted that the appeal was complex and would last half a day.

On two consecutive days in mid-January 1998, there was another appeal hearing. As the applicant's solicitor had become aware that the prosecution had never cited a witness, identified as a witness, to appear at the trial, he had lodged and argued an additional ground of appeal on this point. Following argument, he sought to lodge further additional grounds of appeal. The appeal was continued to a date in mid-February 1998 to enable him to do so.

On a date in mid-February 1998, the High Court held that the failure to hear the evidence of the witness resulted in unfairness and that further evidence should be heard. A designated judge was assigned to hear the evidence. On a late May date in 1998, he fixed a date to take the evidence of the witness. On a late August date in 1998, the hearing was adjourned as the applicant's counsel withdrew from acting on his behalf.

On a mid-October date in 1998, the witness gave evidence. In his note of a late November date in 1998, the designated judge gave his view of the credibility and reliability of the witness. The appeal was relisted for hearing on an early January date in 1999 but the applicant's solicitors wrote to the court requesting that another date, possibly in the following week, be fixed due to the unavailability of counsel.

On a late June date in 1999, the appeal was heard before the High Court.

On a late July date in 1999, the judgment of the court was given by the Lord Justice General, a presiding judge. It dismissed the applicant's appeal.
